sentences.text: My name is Joseph Lipschutz.
L-I-P-S-H-U-T-Z.
My name at birth was Jowzef Lipschutz.
And it was spelled J-O-W-Z-E-F, L-I-P-S-C-H-U-- umlaut-- T-Z.
November 1, 1918.
I was born in Crakow.
K-R-O-W-- I'm sorry.
K-R-O-- umlaut-- W.
No, I think I was wrong.
K-R-O-K-O-- umlaut-- W.
Poland.
My father?
My father's name was Abraham.
He was a merchant.
He had a dual way of making a living.
He had a small store in downtown Crakow, consisting of-- with selling gifts, primarily food-type gifts-- chocolates, fruit baskets, et cetera.
My mother's name was Rozalia.
She was helping out in the store pretty much from Monday to Thursday.
Friday, she was preparing all day long, for the-- for the Sabbath.
And she did not work Saturday and Sunday.
The store was closed.
No, I do not.
Predominantly Polish.
But my parents spoke Yiddish to each other if they did not want us to understand what they're saying.
Well, of course I do.
Our home was an apartment consisting of three rooms, a kitchen, and a small hallway.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- my parents occupied one of the rooms, which was the last room in the apartment.
And the other room was kind of a dining room.
But it had two beds in it.
And one more room, which was-- we used to call it a salon.
But it was, like, kind of a living room.
And there was also two beds in that.
The kitchen consisted of coal-burn oven, and a small gas-burning stove with two burners.
Two tables, one for [NON-ENGLISH] and one for [NON-ENGLISH].
And we had a maid who slept in the little hallway.
My parents, or my father especially was, quite religious.
Even though he was not Hasidic.
But he observed all the holidays, and did not work on Saturday.
Did not carry any money with him on Saturday.
Yes, I think we were quite religious.
Shabbat dinner started usually Friday evening.
My mother baked all Friday, cooked.
Usually there was fish, carp, if I remember, which I did not like.
I-- I didn't eat it then and I wouldn't eat it now.
Usually a chicken soup, with some kind of noodles or some stuff like that.
And of course, a chicken and oh, a roast for dinner.
And then, of course, some kind of a fruit dessert.
This is going back to a time when I was very young.
Things have, of course, changed.
And we did not do nearly as well later.
Oh boy.
I was a child who was interested in everything as long as it was sport.
Everything else was unimportant.
I didn't have any problems.
I mean, I was not a rebellious child.
But I wanted to have things my way.
I played soccer, ping pong.
You name it, I did it.
I had two brothers and a sister.
My older brother, Samuel, was about a year older than I am.
My sister was a couple years younger.
And my youngest brother was two more years younger.
I do not remember that we really played together when we were small.
Unfortunately, my memory doesn't reach that far.
I do remember that we were all going out with my parents regularly, every Sunday afternoon, to a park.
Outside Crakow, my father used to hire what you would call a taxi.
But it was actually a horse and a buggy.
And we used to ride out of town, spend time in the park, play games together with my parents.
Had kind of a supper in the park, usually-- if I remember correctly-- consisting of raspberries and sour cream, or strawberries and sour cream, stuff like that.
This is how I remember it.
And we went back home.
And this was our weekly outing.
No.
I do not remember.
I am kind of aware that two of my four grandparents were still alive after I was born.
But they must have died much before or before the time that my memory can reach.
Well, I was going to a school which was [INAUDIBLE] the grammar school.
It was only, oh, but one house away from there where we lived, on a corner of two streets in Crakow.
But we're so close that we could hear the bell ringing while we were still in the apartment.
And I was in that school for five years.
And it was the original grammar school from which I went to high school.
If I did in the grammar school, I don't remember that.
I did definitely experience a lot of anti-Semitism in high school.
But this is probably-- to a large degree-- because I wound up a high school which graduated mostly priests, Catholic-- Roman Catholic priests.
And there were only two or three Jews in each class.
And we had to leave most of the time when the Roman Catholic priests came to teach their students.
We were not allowed to be present.
Well, it was both.
We had no right to choose.
We could not be there.
Therefore, I can't say that I would rather be there.
Probably not.
We enjoyed being outside.
We were given credit for the time anyway.
Some did.
Some didn't.
I guess some are-- some were very anti-Semitic.
And they wouldn't associate with you at all.
And there were a few of them which we were reasonably friendly with.
Yes, all of them at that time.
Well, when I entered the high school, and as I said, I entered this high school because my father took too much time to decide whether I should go to high school at all.
And when he finally decided I should, the only opening that was available that was in that high school, which, as I said before, graduated predominantly Roman Catholic people, who became priests.
This high school specialized in courses suitable for priesthood.
They taught Latin from the first grade.
As a matter of fact, there was eight years of Latin.
And Greek from the, I think, fifth grade.
There were four years of Greek.
High school was eight years.
And I was there till I was 18.
Oh yes, I was.
I was playing sports during all that time, and even after that.
For a while, I was active in-- in Akiba.
This is-- was a Zionist organization whose intention was to train people as halutzim, to get to go to Israel, to Palestine at that time.
I did not.
My-- my older brother very much wanted to go to Israel.
My-- my father actually prevented him from going.
Otherwise, probably, he would have been alive by now.
After high school, I went for one year to a technical school.
I was-- I-- I find it very difficult to relate that to a school in United States.
But it was higher than high school.
And it was not a college.
It was not a university.
Maybe some kind of a sophisticated vocational school, or so.
And I was studying engineering.
At least, the beginning of engineering.
Because this was only one year.
During high school and after that, I earned some extra money by working as a furrier for my mother's distant cousin after the school hours.
And I earned quite a nice money.
Well, there were many signs of danger, but not danger to my life.
Danger of being beaten up.
Danger of not being allowed to associate with people or organizations that I would love-- like-- have liked to do.
And we had quite a few encounters with non-Jewish people who didn't like, for instance, to lose a game in soccer.
And there was a fight after-- after the game.
And at times it became quite serious.
I will you give you one instance.
We played soccer outside the city on a soccer field.
And there was a well from which we were allowed to drink water.
And we were all in line to-- to get water from the well.
And suddenly, a big non-Jewish fellow came up, pushed me aside, and he's going to drink ahead of me.
Well, one of my friends who was very strong-- I was not-- stepped up to him.
And said to him, no, he was here.
He pointing to-- at me.
He was here first, and he will drink first.
And as the fight ensued, and my friend punched the other fellow in his-- in his mouth and broke a couple teeth-- a couple of his teeth.
This resulted in a instant pogrom.
Hundreds upon hundreds of non-Jewish people, the Catholic people, Pollacks, gathered.
And they were ready to beat us up.
But we, of course, run away.
And they were after us.
We managed to escape, even though this is a very long story, how-- how it all happened.
I can tell it, yes.
We were running on that-- what was a busy walkway.
And they were behind us.
And suddenly, a fellow who we knew as being one of the tough Jewish guys stopped us and said, what are you running for?
Why?
What happened?
So we told him.
He said, don't worry.
I will take care of them.
And he-- he put us on the trolley car.
No.
I'm sorry.
He stopped the Polish people.
Because he had two knives in his hands.
And he said, now you come-- come to me one step further.
And they didn't dare.
They turned away.
Turned away, he put us on the trolley car, and we went home.
No, I do not.
Well, in 1939, the war started.
This was when I was 20 years old.
The war started in Crakow, in-- Friday, September, 1939.
And it started by some German planes and bombs falling over Crakow.
Not very many.
But we realized that the war has started.
Even though we have been aware of the fact that the war is about to start any day, it was not a surprise.
It was just surprising that it just happened on that day.
And many rumors started circling around.
And we were told that the Germans are coming from the West.
Crakow was approximately 100 kilometers west of the German-- east of the German border.
Excuse me.
East of the German border.
So we decided that on Sunday morning, that we will leave Crakow and walk east, towards the eastern part of Poland.
Most young Jewish boys, even middle-aged Jewish boys were walking east.
And also, many non-Jewish people.
My mother and my-- and my sister were left behind.
My father was dead.
He died the year before that.
And we were walking on highways towards east.
We stopped at the first place, about 35 kilometers east of Crakow.
The name Bochnia, B-O-C-H-N-I-A, where my father had an acquaintance.
We rode there.
We knocked on his doors.
And he allowed us to sleep over, on the floor, in-- in his hallway.
At about 4- 5 o'clock in the morning, he knocked on the door.
And he said, you better get out and-- and go further east.
Because the Germans also coming from the south.
By that time, Germans have occupied Czechoslovakia, which is south of Poland.
And therefore, they were coming from the west, as well as from the south.
We had to quickly get organized.
So we started going a little bit more northeast rather than east, to try to get away from both of them, the ones coming from the West, as well as the ones coming from the south.
Being on the road at that time, with thousands and thousands of other people, it was very difficult to get any food.
It was very difficult to get, even, water.
When you let the bucket of a well down into the well, you brought up a bucket of mud.
There was no water in the wells.
We actually-- people actually have drunk the well dry.
So we decided that we gonna-- not gonna stay on the road.
There were three of us, my two brothers and I.
And they will be going through the villages, rather than through the highway.
And we found it much easier.
Because there was water in the wells.
And there was also many different things right in the field, which you could pull and eat.
And we ate it all-- carrots, raw tomatoes, raw beets-- as bad as they were-- and stuff like that.
And we eventually wound up in eastern Poland, and crossed the river Bug, B-U-G, and wound up in the part of Poland which was, at that point, occupied by the Russians.
So actually, we were in a Russian-occupied part of Poland.
From there, we tried to go to a town on the Romanian-- Romanian and-- and Hungarian border, Tarnopol, T-A-R-N-O-P-O-L.
Because we were told that the border is open, and they're allowing people to go through, and wind up in Italy, and hopefully, eventually, in Palestine.
But when we arrived there, because there was no good transportation.
Most of the bridge was walking.
We had realized that the border was not open.
And we had to just turn back.
To turn back, we went to a farm in eastern Poland, under the Russian rule at that time, which was in a-- a-- in a town, known Rava Ruska, capital R-A-V-A, R-- capital R-U-S-K-A.
And this farm-- donated at one time by wealthy Jews in Crakow-- was training Halutzim in agriculture so they would go to Palestine.

Since my older brother was one of the [NON-ENGLISH], or managers in the-- in Akiba, and the farm was controlled by Akiba, we were allowed to-- when we arrived there, we were allowed to move in there and stay with them.
But with the next few days, so many people arrived and stayed with us, that it was more than 100 of us there.
Well, the farm was only intended for about 25 or 30 people at a time.
Therefore, the farm could not support all that group of people.
We had to divide into groups, some earning money in one way or the other, and others, working on the farm.
I was-- I and two other fellows were assigned to do business in-- in the city, in the big city, which was Lwow.
L-W-O-- apostrophe-- W.
We were going to Lwow, carrying various farm products.
Not only from our farm, but also organized-- which is the word which was used at that time-- for stealing and-- and/or buying, and carrying it to the city for sale.
The stuff consisted of chickens, live or dead, turkeys, live or dead, and cheeses, kielbasi, et cetera.
One-- one situation was where we carried a sack full of live chickens into the city.
And it was getting rather late in the evening.
We knocked on different doors in the Jewish section.
And we tried to sell them.
And I remember one lady said to me, well, I would buy one.
But you would have to kill it for me.
So I said, well, no choice.
Give me a knife.
She gave me a knife.
She opened the door to the back stairway.
I stepped on the chicken's head, and cut it.
I cut off his neck.
Maybe it's not pleasant to listen to these things.
But there will be worse than that.
No, I was not in danger just going to the city.
I was in danger with my activities in the city.
Selling goods, even-- even food, was forbidden.
Food was only available on ration cards.
And as a matter of fact, there was, at one time, a situation where I bought a large amount-- I think 100 pounds of Polish kielbasi.
And we divided it among the three of us.
And we carried it in the back sacks to-- to the city.
And we stayed on a corner-- stood on a corner of a street, and selling it by the-- by the piece.
It was so much for a piece of kielbasi, whatever, eight inches long, whatever.
And I was arrested.
Because that was against the law.
I was taken to a police station.
And I was interviewed.
And I had money in my little pocket in my slacks, which they didn't find.
It was an odd-- odd way of having pockets inside here.
It was small enough to fold money.
And always, whatever I paid-- got paid, they didn't find the money, anyway.
But I was now in trouble, to go to jail.
Suddenly, a double door opens up in the police station.
And a fellow who is maybe five feet tall, in boots with two guns on his [INAUDIBLE], seven watches on his arms walks in.
And looks at me.
He says, Joe, what are you doing here?
And I didn't know.
And I don't know through today, who he was.
I suspect that I know he was one of the Communists who lived in Crakow before the war, and knew me from somewhere, whether from a sports club, or from whatever.
And he asked the policeman there, what-- what-- what you keeping him for?
And they told him.
And he said, well, just let him go.
And they did.
Because he knew me.
And he saved my skin.
At that time, Crakow was already occupied?
Crakow was occupied by the Germans.
My mother and my sister still lived in apartment which we lived originally.
No.
I was not able to communicate with them.

But in December of 1939-- or might have been beginning of January in 1940, we-- the two brothers and I-- had decided some-- one of us has to go back and try to bring my mother and my sister out, in order to get out of the German rule.
We already begun to hear what is happening to Jews in the occupied part of Poland.
And of course, since I was the tomboy, I was the-- I-- I was selected to go.
So I smuggled myself across the border and went back to Crakow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To retract here, when we left, September the 3rd, from Crakow, it was still summer.
It was very hot.
And the two brothers and I had only with us very lightweight clothing, sandals, no heavy shoes.
And winters in Poland were quite harsh.
So I decided to bring my mother, my-- for my sister, and the winter clothing, whatever I can carry with, in big-- two big suitcases.
Again, across the river Bug.
But you had to smuggle yourself across the border.
This was not something that you-- that you did, like you travel today, you put suitcases on and you-- and you go.
When you arrive, they give you the suitcases.
So but my mother and my sister would not go.
They thought that they are safer in their apartment in which they lived so many years.
Yes, there is anti-Semitism.
But nobody-- at that point, at least-- is-- badgering them to the point that they would be scared.
People believed that the war would be over in two or three months.
They decided to stay.
So I had no choice but to take the two suitcases with all the winter clothing.
I had the shoe makers put new soles on winter shoes, metal nail-like spikes on the shoes, so they would last longer.
And I smuggled myself across the river Bug, back into the farm.
And I arrived there, sometimes in February, 1940.
We were there.
At that point, the Russians were coming into the farm.
And they were against a Zionist farm existing in their midst.
And they said that they going to liquidate it.
Since we knew that they-- they liquidated, they would all be sent out further east, deeper into the Russian-- into Russia.
We decided, again, that we have to get my mother and my sister out of-- out of the Crakow.
And we already heard, more and more, of the horrible stories that were happening to Jews.
But predominantly, at that time, to males.
They did not bother, so much, women.
They did have to sweep for the streets, and clean toilets, and things like that.
But they-- but nobody was talking about taking somebody's life.
So then-- then, I again smuggled myself into the German part [INAUDIBLE] by Poland.
And this became harder each time.
Because the borders were more guarded.
As a matter of fact, that trip was extremely difficult.

It was difficult to the point that I spent two days and three nights in bushes on-- on the Russian side of the river, trying to get across while I saw Russian border guards and German border guards on the other side of the river, walking along and had to find a time at which I might be able to safely cross.
I was with two other people.
We found such a time.
And this is cutting, making it very short.
This is a long story in its own.
OK.
